meta.title: Session Clone Design
meta.subtitle: sisyphus clone — Technical Architecture
meta.summary: Design for `sisyphus clone "goal"` which duplicates a running session's accumulated knowledge (context, prompts, reports, snapshots) into a new independent session with a different goal. True fork — no parent-child hierarchy, no cross-session communication. Clone spawns at cycle N+1 in strategy mode with programmatic orientation.

sections.name: Protocol & Routing
sections.goal: Understand the new protocol types, history events, and server dispatch path
sections.context:
Data flow through the system:

  CLI (clone.ts)
    │ reads SISYPHUS_SESSION_ID from env
    │ sends clone request over Unix socket
    ▼
  Daemon (server.ts)
    │ resolves partial session ID
    │ looks up cwd from tracking map
    ▼
  SessionManager (session-manager.ts)
    │ validates source (exists, not completed)
    │ delegates filesystem work to state.ts
    │ creates tmux session
    │ spawns orchestrator
    ▼
  Orchestrator (orchestrator.ts)
    │ cycle N+1, strategy mode
    │ receives clone orientation in user prompt
    ▼
  Running clone session

Follows the same pattern as `start`: CLI → daemon → session-manager → orchestrator.

items.title: Clone request/response types
items.content:
Add to the `Request` union in `src/shared/protocol.ts:4`:

```typescript
| { type: 'clone'; sessionId: string; goal: string; context?: string; name?: string; strategy?: boolean }
```

Response uses existing `Response` type — on success:
```typescript
{ ok: true, data: { sessionId: string; tmuxSessionName: string } }
```

This mirrors the `start` response shape (server.ts:180). The `sessionId` field here is the SOURCE session being cloned. The response returns the CLONE's new session ID.

Fields map to requirements:
- `sessionId` → REQ-001 (source session from env)
- `goal` → REQ-001 (positional arg)
- `context` → REQ-002 (`-c, --context`)
- `name` → REQ-002 (`-n, --name`)
- `strategy` → REQ-002 (`--strategy`)
items.agentNotes: Follows existing patterns exactly. No new Response type needed — the existing `{ ok: true, data?: Record<string, unknown> }` shape works.

items.title: History event types
items.content:
Add two new values to the `HistoryEventType` union in `src/shared/history-types.ts:3`:

```typescript
| 'session-cloned'
| 'cloned-from'
```

Event data shapes:

**`session-cloned`** — emitted on the SOURCE session:
```json
{ "cloneSessionId": "<uuid>", "cloneGoal": "<goal text>" }
```

**`cloned-from`** — emitted on the CLONE session:
```json
{ "sourceSessionId": "<uuid>", "sourceGoal": "<source goal text>" }
```

Both use `emitHistoryEvent()` (fire-and-forget, errors swallowed). REQ-023 requires both events.
items.agentNotes: These are audit-trail only — no daemon behavior depends on them. Data shapes aren't type-enforced (HistoryEvent.data is Record<string, unknown>), but keeping them consistent with existing event data patterns.

items.title: Server routing for clone request
items.agentNotes: The tracking map registration mirrors startSession's handler exactly. The unknownSessionError call satisfies REQ-022. cwd comes from the source session's tracking entry — clone inherits the same working directory.

sections.name: Directory Cloning Algorithm
sections.goal: Understand what files get copied, how session ID replacement works, and what gets created fresh
sections.context:
Session directory layout (source → clone):

  .sisyphus/sessions/{sourceId}/        .sisyphus/sessions/{cloneId}/
  ├── state.json          ──────►       ├── state.json (new)
  ├── goal.md             ──────►       ├── goal.md (new goal)
  ├── initial-prompt.md   ──────►       ├── initial-prompt.md (new goal)
  ├── roadmap.md          ──────►       ├── roadmap.md (seed template)
  ├── strategy.md         ─ ─ ─►       ├── strategy.md (only with --strategy)
  ├── context/            ══════►       ├── context/ (deep copy + ID replace)
  ├── prompts/            ══════►       ├── prompts/ (deep copy + ID replace)
  ├── reports/            ══════►       ├── reports/ (deep copy + ID replace)
  ├── snapshots/          ══════►       ├── snapshots/ (deep copy + ID replace)
  └── logs/               ──────►       └── logs/ (fresh empty)

  ══════► = recursive copy with session ID replacement
  ──────► = created fresh (not copied)
  ─ ─ ─► = conditional copy

items.title: Directory copy and fresh file creation
items.agentNotes: cpSync is Node 16.7+ (we require Node 22). It's already used in state.ts for snapshot operations. The function is synchronous — no session lock needed since the clone directory doesn't exist yet (no concurrent access possible).

items.title: Session ID replacement in text files
items.description: replaceIdInDir() — recursive text file ID substitution

proposal.title: Null-byte heuristic for text detection
proposal.description: Read as Buffer, skip files containing \0 bytes. Simple, no extension allowlist to maintain, handles all text encodings.

alternatives.title: Extension allowlist
alternatives.description: Only process .md, .json, .txt, .ts files. More predictable but requires maintenance as new file types appear in session dirs.

lenses.reliability: Null-byte heuristic is robust for UTF-8 text — no false positives for typical session content
lenses.maintenance: No allowlist to update when new file types are added to session directories
lenses.performance: Reads every file once — acceptable for session dirs (typically <100 files)

items.agentNotes: readFileSync already imported in state.ts. The replaceAll method is available in Node 22. Buffer.includes(0) is O(n) in file size but session files are small.

sections.name: State Model
sections.goal: Understand how the clone's state.json is constructed from the source session
sections.context:
The clone state.json is a hybrid: new identity + inherited history.

  Source state.json                Clone state.json
  ┌─────────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────────┐
  │ id: source-uuid     │ ──X──►  │ id: clone-uuid      │  ← new
  │ task: old goal      │ ──X──►  │ task: new goal      │  ← new
  │ status: active      │ ──X──►  │ status: active      │  ← fresh
  │ createdAt: old      │ ──X──►  │ createdAt: now      │  ← fresh
  │ activeMs: 12345     │ ──X──►  │ activeMs: 0         │  ← reset
  │ completedAt: ...    │ ──X──►  │ completedAt: undef  │  ← cleared
  │                     │         │                     │
  │ agents: [...]       │ ═════►  │ agents: [...]       │  ← preserved*
  │ orchestratorCycles  │ ═════►  │ orchestratorCycles  │  ← preserved
  │ messages: [...]     │ ═════►  │ messages: [...]     │  ← preserved
  │ model: opus         │ ═════►  │ model: opus         │  ← preserved
  │ cwd: /project       │ ═════►  │ cwd: /project       │  ← preserved
  └─────────────────────┘         └─────────────────────┘

  * agents with status='running' normalized to 'killed'

items.title: Clone state.json construction
items.agentNotes: The shallow spread on agents/cycles/messages is sufficient — their fields are all primitives or flat objects. No nested references to worry about. The atomicWrite pattern matches createSession at state.ts:74.

items.title: Agent status normalization
items.description: How inherited running agents are handled in clone state
items.content:
**Problem**: `allAgentsDone()` in `src/daemon/agent.ts:492` checks `session.agents.filter(a => a.status === 'running').length === 0`. If the clone inherits agents with `status: 'running'` from the source, the daemon would block orchestrator respawning — those agents have no panes in the clone's tmux session.

**Solution**: When building clone state, any agent with `status === 'running'` gets:
```json
{
  "status": "killed",
  "completedAt": "<clone creation timestamp>",
  "killedReason": "inherited from source session"
}
```

All other statuses (`completed`, `crashed`, `killed`, `lost`) are preserved as-is — they're already terminal and won't confuse the daemon.

**Why `killed` and not `completed`**: The agent didn't actually complete its work in the clone's context. Using `killed` is semantically accurate (work was cut short) and distinguishable from agents that genuinely finished. The `killedReason` field makes the origin clear.

**Impact on agent counter**: `resetAgentCounterFromState()` scans all agent IDs regardless of status. If the source had `agent-001` through `agent-005`, the clone's new agents start at `agent-006`. This is correct — avoids ID collisions in reports/ and state.

proposal.title: Mark running agents as 'killed' with explanatory reason
proposal.description: Semantically accurate (work was interrupted for this session), doesn't confuse allAgentsDone(), and the killedReason field provides audit trail.

alternatives.title: Mark as 'completed'
alternatives.description: Simpler but semantically wrong — these agents didn't complete work in the clone context. Could confuse orchestrator interpreting history.

alternatives.title: Strip all non-terminal agents from clone
alternatives.description: Loses history of in-progress work, which the orchestrator needs for situational awareness.

lenses.correctness: killed + reason is semantically accurate and prevents daemon confusion
lenses.history fidelity: Preserves all agent records — orchestrator sees full picture of what was in-flight
lenses.daemon safety: allAgentsDone() returns true since no agents have status='running'

items.agentNotes: This is the critical safety item. Without normalization, a clone from an active session with running agents would have a permanently broken daemon tracking state.

sections.name: Session Manager Flow
sections.goal: Understand the complete cloneSession() orchestration in session-manager.ts
sections.context:
cloneSession() follows startSession() (session-manager.ts:90) closely:

  cloneSession(sourceId, cwd, goal, context?, name?, strategy?)
    │
    ├─ 1. Validate source session
    │     ├─ getSession() — exists?
    │     └─ status !== 'completed'?  (REQ-019)
    │
    ├─ 2. Generate clone UUID
    │
    ├─ 3. Filesystem (state.ts)
    │     ├─ cloneSessionDir()  — copy dirs, replace IDs, fresh files
    │     └─ createCloneState() — build state.json
    │
    ├─ 4. Model config (same as startSession)
    │     └─ updateSession({ model, launchConfig })
    │
    ├─ 5. Tmux session
    │     ├─ createSession(tmuxName, cwd)
    │     ├─ initSessionMeta()
    │     └─ updateSessionTmux()
    │
    ├─ 6. Track & spawn
    │     ├─ trackSession()
    │     ├─ spawnOrchestrator(... orientation, 'strategy')
    │     ├─ updateTrackedWindow()
    │     └─ killPane(initialPaneId)
    │
    ├─ 7. History events (REQ-023)
    │     ├─ emitHistoryEvent(sourceId, 'session-cloned', ...)
    │     └─ emitHistoryEvent(cloneId, 'cloned-from', ...)
    │
    ├─ 8. Haiku naming (fire-and-forget)
    │
    └─ 9. Companion + recomputeDots()

items.title: cloneSession() implementation
items.description: Full session-manager.ts function following startSession pattern
items.agentNotes:
resetAgentCounterFromState is critical here — without it, new agents spawned by the clone's orchestrator would start at agent-001, colliding with inherited agents. The call at step 6 ensures the counter picks up from the source's last agent number.

Note: pruneOldSessions and pruneHistory are called at the end, same as startSession. This prevents session accumulation.

sections.name: Orchestrator Orientation
sections.goal: Understand how the clone's orchestrator receives fork context and starts in strategy mode
sections.context:
spawnOrchestrator flow for clone:

  spawnOrchestrator(cloneId, cwd, windowId, message, 'strategy')
    │
    ├─ mode = forceMode ?? (lastCycle?.mode ?? 'strategy')
    │         = 'strategy'  (forced)
    │
    ├─ basePrompt = loadOrchestratorPrompt(cwd, cloneId, 'strategy')
    │
    ├─ formattedState = formatStateForOrchestrator(session, 'strategy')
    │   ├─ ## Goal  ← reads clone's goal.md (new goal)
    │   ├─ ## Context  ← clone's context/ dir (inherited + replaced IDs)
    │   ├─ ### Most Recent Cycle  ← source's last cycle agents/reports
    │   ├─ ## Strategy  ← empty (not copied by default)
    │   └─ ## Roadmap  ← seed template
    │
    ├─ userPrompt = formattedState + orientation message
    │   └─ ## Continuation Instructions
    │      └─ [clone orientation text]
    │
    └─ cycle N+1 recorded in state

items.title: forceMode parameter on spawnOrchestrator
items.agentNotes: This is a 2-line change with zero impact on existing callers. The alternative — modifying the last cycle's mode in clone state — would corrupt the inherited history.

items.title: Clone orientation message content
items.description: What the orchestrator sees on its first cycle
items.content:
The orientation is passed as the `message` parameter to `spawnOrchestrator()`. It appears in the user prompt under `## Continuation Instructions`:

```markdown
## Continuation Instructions

The user resumed this session with new instructions:

This is a **cloned session**, forked from an existing session.

Source session: {sourceId}
Previous goal: {sourceGoal}

You have full access to the previous session's context/, reports/,
and cycle history. Use them as background for your work.

Your new goal is: {newGoal}

### Additional Context

{context text, if --context was provided}

**Important**: The source session continues independently.
It is the other session's responsibility. You do not need to monitor it.

### Next Steps
1. Review inherited context/ and reports/
2. Write strategy.md for your approach
3. Update roadmap.md with your work plan
4. Begin delegating work to agents
```

The `formatStateForOrchestrator()` already provides:
- `## Goal` → the new goal (from clone's goal.md)
- `## Context` → inherited context/ dir reference
- `### Most Recent Cycle` → last cycle's agent reports
- `## Strategy` → empty (unless --strategy was used)
- `## Roadmap` → seed template

So the orchestrator sees the full picture: what was done before (history), what's available (context), and what to do next (orientation).

REQ-011 (programmatic orientation) and REQ-012 (context passthrough) are both satisfied.
items.agentNotes: The 'The user resumed this session with new instructions:' prefix comes from spawnOrchestrator's message handling at orchestrator.ts:377. This framing is close enough — the orchestrator understands it's getting new direction. The orientation body makes the clone nature explicit.

sections.name: CLI Command
sections.goal: Understand the clone command's argument parsing, output format, and registration
sections.context:
CLI invocation pattern:

  # From an orchestrator or agent pane (has SISYPHUS_SESSION_ID env)
  sisyphus clone "implement the auth system" \
    --context "focus on OAuth2 flow" \
    --strategy \
    --name auth-clone

  # Output (this IS the orchestrator's prompt continuation):
  Session cloned successfully.
    Clone: {cloneId}
    Tmux:  {tmuxSessionName}

  The cloned session now owns: "implement the auth system"
  This is the other session's responsibility.
  You do not need to monitor it.

  Update your scope:
  - Remove cloned work from goal.md
  - Update roadmap.md to reflect reduced scope
  - Update strategy.md if approach changes

items.title: clone.ts command implementation
items.agentNotes: This follows the continue.ts pattern (reads session ID from env) more than the start.ts pattern (which takes cwd from env). The output format is carefully designed as orchestrator guidance per REQ-013/REQ-015 — no `sisyphus status` or `tail -f` commands that would encourage monitoring.

items.title: CLI output as orchestrator guidance
items.description: Output format satisfying REQ-013 and REQ-015
items.content:
The CLI output is consumed by the orchestrator (or agent) that called `sisyphus clone`. It's designed as a prompt, not human-facing info.

**REQ-013** (behavioral guidance): The output explicitly states:
> This is the other session's responsibility. You do not need to monitor it.

This prevents the orchestrator from trying to track the clone's progress.

**REQ-015** (next-step instructions): The output instructs:
> Update your scope:
> - Remove cloned work from goal.md
> - Update roadmap.md to reflect reduced scope
> - Update strategy.md if approach changes

This ensures the source session narrows its own scope after cloning off work.

**What's NOT in the output** (intentional omissions):
- No `sisyphus status <cloneId>` — would encourage monitoring
- No `tmux attach -t` — same reason
- No file paths — the orchestrator knows its own files
- No `tail -f ~/.sisyphus/daemon.log` — not relevant
items.agentNotes: Compare with start.ts output (lines 54-73) which includes monitor and control commands. The clone output deliberately omits all of that. 'Every output is a prompt' — the requirements are explicit about this design philosophy.

sections.name: Error Handling
sections.goal: Map every error path from requirements to its implementation point
sections.context:
Error paths through the system:

  CLI (clone.ts)
    ├─ SISYPHUS_SESSION_ID not set → exit 1     (REQ-003)
    └─ sendRequest failure → exit 1

  Server (server.ts)
    └─ sessionTrackingMap miss → unknownSessionError  (REQ-022)

  SessionManager (session-manager.ts)
    ├─ source.status === 'completed' → throw      (REQ-019)
    ├─ invalid name → throw
    └─ tmux name collision → throw

  State (state.ts)
    └─ source dir missing → getSession throws     (REQ-022)

items.title: Error path matrix
items.description: Complete mapping of requirements to error handling code
items.content:
| Requirement | Error Condition | Where Caught | Error Message | Exit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-003 | `SISYPHUS_SESSION_ID` not set | `clone.ts` | "SISYPHUS_SESSION_ID not set. Run this from an orchestrator or agent pane." | CLI exit 1 |
| REQ-019 | Source session `status === 'completed'` | `session-manager.ts` cloneSession() | "Cannot clone completed session {id}. Use `sisyphus continue` to reactivate it first." | Daemon throws → CLI exit 1 |
| REQ-022 | Session ID not in tracking map | `server.ts` unknownSessionError() | "Unknown session: {id}. Run `sisyphus list --all` to see available sessions." | `{ok:false}` → CLI exit 1 |
| REQ-022 | Session dir missing on disk | `state.ts` getSession() | ENOENT on state.json read | Daemon throws → CLI exit 1 |
| REQ-017 | Clone from active session | `session-manager.ts` | No error — proceeds normally | N/A |
| REQ-018 | Clone from paused session | `session-manager.ts` | No error — proceeds normally | N/A |
| REQ-020 | Empty context/ directory | `state.ts` cloneSessionDir() | No error — cpSync copies empty dir | N/A |
| REQ-021 | Multiple clones from same source | All layers | No error — each clone gets fresh UUID | N/A |
| (implicit) | Invalid `--name` format | `session-manager.ts` | "Invalid session name: only alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores" | Daemon throws → CLI exit 1 |
| (implicit) | Tmux session name collision | `session-manager.ts` | "Tmux session already exists" | Daemon throws → CLI exit 1 |

**Active session cloning (REQ-017)**: The source session's running agents are irrelevant to the clone. The clone copies state.json (with agent normalization) and files. Running agents in the source continue working — they don't know or care that a clone was made.

**Paused session cloning (REQ-018)**: Same as active. Source stays paused. Clone starts active with its own tmux session.

**Multiple clones (REQ-021)**: Each call generates a fresh UUID. File copies are independent. No cross-clone coordination needed.
items.agentNotes: Error messages follow existing patterns. The unknownSessionError is reused from server.ts:76. The completed-session error suggests `sisyphus continue` as a recovery path, which is the existing mechanism for reactivating completed sessions.

sections.name: Implementation File Map
sections.goal: Complete list of files to create or modify, with the nature of each change
sections.context:
All changes organized by layer:

  shared/
    ├── protocol.ts        (modify — add clone request type)
    └── history-types.ts   (modify — add 2 event types)

  daemon/
    ├── state.ts           (modify — add cloneSessionDir, createCloneState,
    │                                    replaceIdInDir)
    ├── session-manager.ts (modify — add cloneSession function)
    ├── server.ts          (modify — add clone case in handleRequest)
    └── orchestrator.ts    (modify — add forceMode parameter)

  cli/
    ├── commands/clone.ts  (create — new command file)
    └── index.ts           (modify — register clone command)

items.title: Complete file change manifest
items.description: Every file touched, with line-level change descriptions
items.content:
**New files (1):**

1. `src/cli/commands/clone.ts` — ~55 lines. `registerClone(program)` function.

**Modified files (7):**

1. `src/shared/protocol.ts:4` — Add `| { type: 'clone'; sessionId: string; goal: string; context?: string; name?: string; strategy?: boolean }` to Request union.

2. `src/shared/history-types.ts:3` — Add `'session-cloned' | 'cloned-from'` to HistoryEventType union.

3. `src/daemon/state.ts` — Add 3 functions:
   - `replaceIdInDir()` (~20 lines, private)
   - `cloneSessionDir()` (~35 lines, exported)
   - `createCloneState()` (~45 lines, exported)
   - Add `strategyPath` to paths import

4. `src/daemon/session-manager.ts` — Add `cloneSession()` (~90 lines, exported). Uses existing imports (`uuidv4`, `state`, `orchestrator`, `tmux`, etc.) plus `resetAgentCounterFromState` from agent.ts.

5. `src/daemon/server.ts:160` — Add `case 'clone'` block (~15 lines) in handleRequest switch.

6. `src/daemon/orchestrator.ts:309` — Add `forceMode?: string` parameter, change mode resolution line. 2-line diff.

7. `src/cli/index.ts` — Add import for `registerClone`, add `registerClone(program)` call. 2 lines.

**Total**: ~260 lines of new code across 8 files. No test files listed — implementation agent should add tests to `src/__tests__/` following existing patterns (see `state.test.ts`, `session-logic.test.ts`).
items.agentNotes: The Haiku naming block in cloneSession is ~35 lines of boilerplate identical to startSession's. An implementation agent might extract a shared helper, but that's an implementation detail — not a design decision. The design just requires the same behavior.
